title: Google Search is now fully powered by Gemini 3.5 Flash — the ten blue links are gone by default
subtitle: Google has moved its core Search product to an AI-generated summary page for every query, with agentic follow-ups and image or video queries built in, and says AI Mode has surpassed one billion monthly active users.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: As of July 10, Google Search's default results page is generated by Gemini 3.5 Flash rather than composed from a ranked list of ten blue links. Every query now returns an AI-generated summary — with source links embedded inline in the answer instead of listed separately below it — and users can chain follow-up questions, submit images or short videos as queries, and hand off longer tasks to what Google calls 'information agents' inside the same session. Google framed the shift as the most significant change to Search in more than 25 years.

The backend rebuild pairs Gemini 3.5 Flash with a new agentic framework Google is calling Antigravity, which lets Search compile real-time web data on the fly and, for some queries, generate small interactive mini-applications directly inside the results — for example, a currency converter, a comparison table, or a scenario calculator specific to what was asked. Google also disclosed that its conversational 'AI Mode' — introduced just over a year ago as an opt-in — has crossed one billion monthly active users, with query volume doubling every quarter since launch. The classic ten-link view remains available as a mode toggle but is no longer the default surface.

The implication for the wider web is what publishers have been warning about for two years: if Search answers the question in the results box, fewer users click through to the underlying page. Google argues that its inline citations preserve attribution and referral traffic to sources, but early third-party measurements of AI Overviews have generally shown decisive click-through drops on informational queries. Combined with Nokia adding an 'AI button' to phones this week and OpenAI's ChatGPT Search continuing to grow, the browsing pattern is visibly shifting from 'query → list → click → read' toward 'query → answer → maybe click.'

Takeaway for learners: this is a good moment to notice how quickly the primary interface to the web changes when the incumbent decides to rewrite it. If you build web content, the SEO playbook you learned last year — target keywords, rank on page one, get the click — is being replaced by a game where the goal is to be one of a handful of sources a summary model chooses to cite. If you build software, expect users to reach your product through natural-language queries handled by an agent, not through your carefully-designed landing page. Both realities favor clarity and structured content over decorated copy.
